Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a routine. I'd tell myself I would study, but then I'd just feel this heaviness and couldn't bring myself to start. Most of my studying happened in bursts when I absolutely had to, usually late at night when the anxiety got bad enough to push me into action. I know I spent around 27 hours a week supposedly studying, but a lot of that time I was just sitting there, trying to focus while my mind wandered to everything else going wrong.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. Some weeks I wouldn't touch anything until a deadline was breathing down my neck, and then I'd panic-study for hours. Other times, I'd have good intentions and try to set a schedule, but life stuff—work, family obligations, just feeling emotionally drained—would throw everything off. There was no consistency, and I think that made everything harder.
Q3: Usually it was panic. Like, I'd suddenly remember an assignment was due or I'd get a notification that something was closing soon. Sometimes a classmate would message me asking about something, and that would remind me I needed to check in. It wasn't like a habit or anything—more like being jolted into action when I couldn't avoid it anymore.
Q4: Not really, no. I tried a few times to make it a daily thing, like checking in every morning, but it never stuck. The LMS felt like this reminder of everything I was falling behind on, so logging in actually made me more anxious. I'd avoid it just to not feel that sinking feeling in my stomach.
Q5: The recorded lectures were helpful because I could listen to them while doing other things or lying in bed when I couldn't bring myself to sit at a desk. I learn better by hearing things anyway, so being able to replay explanations helped. The discussion forums were okay too—seeing other students' questions made me feel less alone in being confused.
Q6: I barely touched the readings and the extra practice materials. I kept telling myself I'd get to them later, but later never came. They felt overwhelming, like this mountain of content I'd never climb. With limited resources, I couldn't always print things out, and reading on a screen for hours just exhausted me.
Q7: If I could see how it directly connected to passing an assignment or the exam, I'd do it. Anything that felt abstract or like 'enrichment' got pushed aside because I was in survival mode. I needed to feel like my limited energy was going toward something that would actually count toward my grade.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. Required ones at least had a deadline pushing me, but optional stuff? I'd look at it and think 'that would be nice,' then immediately feel guilty for not having the bandwidth. It's like the required ones were obligations, and optional ones were just reminders of the student I wished I could be.
Q9: Yes, definitely. I'd download lecture recordings and listen to them on my phone during my commute or while trying to fall asleep. I also borrowed a classmate's notes because I missed so many classes. A lot of my actual learning happened away from my computer, just going over things in my head or talking through concepts with a friend from the peer support group.
Q10: So many times. I'd spend hours reviewing downloaded materials or listening to recordings, but none of that showed up anywhere. Or I'd be processing information while doing chores, mentally going through what I'd learned. The LMS only saw me when I logged in, but my brain was working on course stuff way more than those numbers would suggest.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a lecture video once, and then I'd listen to it three or four more times offline. Same with any PDFs I could save—I'd read them on my phone later when I had time. One login, but hours of studying that happened after.
Q12: There were weeks when everything felt impossible. My anxiety would spike, and I'd just shut down. Sometimes it was financial stress—worrying about making rent, whether I could afford to keep going to school. Other times I just felt so sad and disconnected that opening my laptop felt like lifting a boulder. Those were the weeks my attendance and engagement completely dropped.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted from work or overwhelmed by six online courses at once, the LMS became this thing I dreaded. I'd log in, see all the notifications and overdue items, and feel paralyzed. The confusion from missing classes made it worse—I didn't even know where to start, so sometimes I just didn't.
Q14: More times than I want to admit. I'd open a video and let it play while staring at the wall, or scroll through readings without any of it sinking in. My body was there, technically engaging, but my mind was somewhere else entirely—usually worrying about something or just feeling numb.
Q15: I postponed almost everything important at some point. The bigger the assignment, the more I avoided it. I think it was fear of failing or not being good enough. Starting meant confronting how behind I was, so not starting felt safer somehow, even though it made everything worse.
Q16: Deadlines were the only thing that got me moving. I'd ignore something for weeks, then scramble in the last 15 hours before it was due. My response time was terrible because I was always reacting instead of planning. The deadline pressure was stressful, but without it, I probably wouldn't have submitted anything.
Q17: When I actually got feedback, it helped motivate me for a little while. Seeing that I could do well on assignments—like that 93% score—reminded me I wasn't hopeless. But the good feeling faded quickly, and I'd slip back into avoidance. Negative or no feedback just confirmed the voice in my head saying I was failing.
Q18: The structure was hard for me. Having six courses online meant constant competing deadlines, and I never felt like I could give any one course proper attention. The course itself was okay, but I needed more flexibility or check-ins to stay on track. Without that external accountability, I drifted.
Q19: The recorded lectures made things easier because I could learn on my own time and in my own way. But the amount of reading and the pace of new content being released made it harder. I couldn't keep up, and once I fell behind, catching up felt impossible. More structure around pacing would have helped me.
Q20: That was me sometimes. I'd log in, click around, open things, maybe start watching something—but then I'd get overwhelmed or distracted and never actually complete anything. High activity doesn't mean high productivity. Sometimes it's just someone trying to figure out where to even begin.
Q21: That also sounds like me, honestly. My exam score was decent and I passed, but my LMS activity was low. I did a lot of learning offline—listening to recordings, talking with peers from the support group, reviewing notes. The system didn't capture the studying that actually helped me pass.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show whether you actually understood anything or how you were feeling. They miss the offline work, the mental processing, the conversations with classmates. They definitely can't see the anxiety that makes you avoid logging in altogether.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling connected to it—wanting to understand, not just pass. For me, engagement was rare this semester. When I was engaged, it was usually during peer discussions or when something in a lecture actually clicked. But most of the time, I was just trying to survive.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I was struggling emotionally, or how the peer support actually kept me from dropping out entirely. It wouldn't show the hours I spent listening to lectures while crying, or the relief I felt when a classmate checked in on me. The human stuff—that's what actually mattered, and none of it leaves a data trail.
